title: Carlsen vs Caruana — 2018 Tiebreak
description: Magnus Carlsen squeezes Fabiano Caruana in a Taimanov Sicilian to win Game 3 of the 2018 World Championship rapid tiebreak — annotated by samsh.

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator marks 1.e4 with a "!". When White only needs a draw to clinch the match, why is 1.e4 called the practical choice?

options.text: It steers the game toward the most forcing lines — the surest way to make a draw with White.
options.explanation: Correct. The annotator writes that if you specifically need a draw with White, "1.e4 is absolutely the way to do it. Play the most forcing moves."

options.text: It is the only first move that can fight for an opening advantage.
options.explanation: Not the claim. The annotator says e4, d4, Nf3 and c4 all have "similar chances of fighting for an edge" — 1.e4 is singled out for being the most forcing, not the only good move.

options.text: It wins a pawn by force in the opening.
options.explanation: The annotator makes no such claim; 1.e4 is praised for forcing, drawish practicality, not for winning material.

conceptChecks.hint: Read the last sentence of the annotation.

conceptChecks.prompt: Caruana, who must win, plays 5...Bc5 ("!") instead of the well-known 5...Nf6. Why does the annotator approve?

options.text: 5...Nf6 leads to sterile equality, which is useless to a player who must win — 5...Bc5 keeps the game imbalanced.
options.explanation: Correct. The note on 5...Nf6 says it "ends in sterile equality very quickly... but this game is a bit different" — Caruana needs winning chances, so 5...Bc5 is "the best move under the circumstances."

options.text: 5...Nf6 simply loses a piece to a tactic.
options.explanation: No — the annotator calls the 5...Nf6 line safe and equal, not losing.

options.text: 5...Bc5 sets up an immediate checkmate threat.
options.explanation: The annotator claims no such threat; 5...Bc5 is about keeping tension and winning chances, not forcing mate.

conceptChecks.hint: Compare what each move does to Black's winning chances.

conceptChecks.prompt: What is the point of 14.f4 ("!") according to the annotation?

options.text: It grabs even more space, squeezing Black in Maroczy-bind style.
options.explanation: Correct. The note is simply "White takes even more space," continuing the space-advantage plan the annotator highlighted earlier ("in the style of the Maroczy bind").

options.text: It launches an immediate mating attack on the black king.
options.explanation: Not yet — at this stage the annotator stresses space and a slow squeeze, not a direct attack.

options.text: It wins the c5-pawn by force.
options.explanation: The annotator does not mention winning the c5-pawn; 14.f4 is about space.

conceptChecks.hint: What has White been accumulating all game?

conceptChecks.prompt: Why does the annotator praise 24.g4 ("!") as undoubtedly the best move, even though a draw already clinches the title?

options.text: It expands White's space further, and Carlsen accepts somewhat opening his own king to keep pressing for the win.
options.explanation: Correct. The note credits Magnus "for being willing to somewhat open his king in a game where a draw clinches the World Championship" while "White expands his space advantage further."

options.text: It forces an immediate queen trade into a dead-drawn endgame.
options.explanation: The opposite of the annotator's point — 24.g4 keeps pressing and even risks the king, rather than bailing into a draw.

options.text: It defends against a dangerous Black attack.
options.explanation: Black has no attack here; the annotator notes Black has been "struggling to find any kind of active plan."

conceptChecks.hint: The praise is about ambition, not safety.

conceptChecks.prompt: At move 26 Carlsen chooses 26.e5 rather than the aggressive 26.g5!?. How does the annotator explain the practical decision?

options.text: 26.e5 heads for simplification and a safe result; 26.g5 would go directly for the king — understandable when a draw secures the championship.
options.explanation: Correct. On 26.g5!? the note says "White could have gone directly for the king, but one can hardly blame Magnus for looking for simplification and instant gratification."

options.text: 26.g5 would have simply lost on the spot.
options.explanation: No — the annotator actually says of 26.g5 that "he'd likely have won." It was the more ambitious try, not a blunder.

options.text: 26.e5 forces checkmate within a few moves.
options.explanation: The annotator frames 26.e5 as simplification toward a safe result, not a forced mate.

conceptChecks.hint: What does a draw already guarantee Carlsen here?

conceptChecks.prompt: 41.b4 ("!") comes with the note "Passed pawns must be pushed!". What does the annotator say ultimately decides the game?

options.text: White's passed c-pawn decides the game long before Black's attack can arrive.
options.explanation: Correct. At 43.c6 the annotator writes that "White's c-pawn will decide the game long before any attack has a dream of coming through."

options.text: A direct checkmate of the black king.
options.explanation: Not the annotator's verdict — White wins via the passed pawn, and the note even calls White's king "really hard to imagine" getting mated.

options.text: A fork that wins Black's knight.
options.explanation: No fork is mentioned; the deciding factor is the passed c-pawn racing to promotion.

conceptChecks.hint: Follow the c-pawn over the next few moves.
